speaker: interviewee
text: And he's got to go--

speaker: interviewer
text: He had--

speaker: interviewee
text: --through this

speaker: interviewer
text: --to do it--

speaker: interviewee
text: --all over

speaker: interviewer
text: --again.

speaker: interviewee
text: --again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. And very emotional. He cried a lot.

speaker: interviewee
text: Aw, see?

speaker: interviewer
text: If you cry, it's all right, by the way, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I have been doing-- speaking about it-- you know, 25 years, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you don't--

speaker: interviewee
text: --I don't get so emotional--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --anymore. I do-- I think, mostly when I speak of my father, you know, having to take me to--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know what you're-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --the Nazis-- That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was so terrible. The man was sick. He lives in Palm Aire. He's so-- such a sick man, and he had to do it again the next week-- aware--

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that is painful. That's-- you know, that's negligence-- carelessness.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you see, it's-- it-- anybody can have a mouth.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, it can happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: But after tape two, when she saw she had problems, she should she should have stopped.

speaker: interviewee
text: She should have-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] --turn it a certain way. I lost. So I said, I don't need it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: My goodness. How far am I from a telephone? [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: We have to--

speaker: interviewer
text: With my kids, it's so busy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: They walk around talking to you, and they're doing the dishes, or they're cleaning--

speaker: interviewee
text: What I hate the most-- the telephones in the cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: I look at Ruth?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sh!

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm. Don't look at the camera. Look at me. It's the 3rd of April, 1995; interviewer-- interview with survivor, Rose Kaplowitz; interview-- interviewer is Ruth Resnikoff. We are in Coconut Creek, in the United States. The language is English. And this is tape one.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] It's the 4th of April, 1995. I am Ruth Resnikoff-- Ruth, R-E-S-N-I-K-O-F-F. And I'm interviewing survivor, Rose Kaplowitz-- Rose, K-A-P-L-O-W-I-T-Z. We're in Coconut Creek, in Rose's winter apartment, in Florida, USA. The interview is in English. [PAUSES FOR 18 SECONDS] Rose, tell me your name before you were married. Polish name was [NON-ENGLISH] Zaks. In English it would be Rose Zaks,

speaker: interviewer
text: And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Z-A-K-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your nickname? What did the family call you?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], but it was just Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born on September 6, 1930.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sosnowiec, Poland, which is the southwestern part of Poland, very close to the German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me something about the Jewish life of Sosnowiec.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: --when I look back today, I think I had a very happy childhood. We lived on a street that was mixed. There were Jewish people living there as well as Polish Christians. We children got along-- played together nicely. Our school, which was public school for Jewish children only, was a-- practically across the street from us. So we had always a lot of children. Many times, after school, they all would come and play in our yard all kinds of games. I lived in a three-story building-- that with my father, my mother. I had-- we were six sisters and one brother. And maybe because of the number of children, we had a very happy home life. There was a lot of singing and laughter in our home. And we had a lot of friends. Life was-- the anti-Semitism prevailed in Poland. Somehow, our life was quite normal, especially on our street, where we got along well. We played together. There were all kinds of games that we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's funny that some of the games that I played on a paper with a pencil-- I see my grandchildren today playing on computers. And they-- if I explain them the game-- how do you know it, grandma? So we had a beautiful childhood, I think.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember vividly holidays. I remember my father and neighbors building the sukkah outside. And for each holiday, somehow, the holidays were beautiful. It's like you felt it in the air. And for each holidays, there were special game. For Passover, we would use hazel nuts and grind them, make them into round balls, and play all kinds of games on the ground. We had a happy childhood. I went to school. We had all kinds of activities in school as well, and all kinds of plays. Like for-- mother-- Mother's Day was important in our school, because we would always put up a play. And all the mothers would come to school. And so I have wonderful memories of home. We had a grocery store. And my father was always in the store. My mother was helping, also.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sell to gentiles as well as Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Most of our customers were gentiles. And, as I said, because it was a mixed neighborhood, I was exposed to Christian friends as well as to Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say that your family were observant or less?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. My family was observant, yes. My father was orthodox.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me something about the synagogue? Do you remember the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The synagogue was right down the corner from our street. And I loved to go there, especially on Rosh Hashanah, because my father would blow the shofar. And I would stand next to him and pull on his talis. And I was so proud, like, ah. Oh, that's my father doing it. I was just a little girl, but I was so proud. I thought it was such a great thing. And in school, where I went-- it was a school. It was a public school. But nevertheless, Jewish children in my hometown went to a separate school. We-- the language and everything was Polish. But once a week, we would have a teacher come and give us an hour of religion. But that was all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you remember the population of the town and how many Jews? How many--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. The population was about 100,000 to 110,000 people, and the Jewish population at that time numbered between 28,000 to 30,000 Jews. So we were quite visible in our home town. There was also a huge great synagogue which was gorgeous. But of course, we were not going there, because my father walked to temple, so it had to be around the corner.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- very, very, very slightly-- not much. Why? Because they lived-- well, one grandmother lived in my hometown, but she died, and I don't-- don't have the recollection. As a matter of fact, what's frightening to me-- not only I am forgetting a grandmother, but also, the pictures of my parents are slowly, slowly becoming less and less vivid. And it's a frightening experience for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say you didn't experience any anti-Semitism before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I didn't say that. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: We did, but we learned to live with it, and accept it, and go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of event do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, it was mostly-- I was exposed to anti-Semitism from like my-- my Christian friends, who were my friends, by the way. But every now and then, they would say something derogatory, something negative. But we would speak back to them. And one thing-- we-- or at least family-- members of my family-- we never ran away. If somebody wanted to hit you or something, we stood our ground. So soon, they realized that we are not going to run, so they couldn't frighten us enough. My brother played soccer together with Jewish and Christian friends. So I cannot say that I personally was exposed to anti-Semitism. Though there is something that I'll never forget. In the summer of 1939, we got up one morning, and when my father opened the store, two Christians that we did not know, stood in front of the store with a sign-- this is a Jewish store. Do not shop in a Jewish store. And it was a shocking experience to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were there that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was before the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to go back-- you had a family of seven children.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you fit in? Tell me about your brothers and sisters.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was the second youngest. So I have all older sisters and a brother. And we had a wonderful life, really. Like my sister was a very good skater. She-- my father would tell her if she was skating, she's got to take me along. Of course, she wasn't happy.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: She wanted to play with her older her friends. But sometimes, she had to take me along. Or sometimes, my two older sisters-- my sister Mania and Tola-- went to a movie. And my father and mother said, take her along. So they would. And once-- I'll never forget that-- and I always remind them-- it was a movie that a child under a certain age could not enter. So they told me, [NON-ENGLISH], sit here in front of the movie on the steps and wait for us. I did. I was crying, but I did. And on the way home, they said to me, better don't tell father, or we'll never take you again. [LAUGHS] But we had a wonderful life.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the HaNoar HaTzioni.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. That was in-- or-- a Zionist organization to which members of my family belonged. I was too young. I did not join it until during-- during the war. But it was an organization where Jewish youth would get together. There were many organizations in my home town-- HaNoar HaTzioni, Hashomer, HaDati, Hashomer Hatzair, Gordonia, Betar-- almost every branch of Zionist organization. And they were quite strong.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And while you were growing up, Hitler was strengthening his power so close to you in Germany.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the effect that had on your family? Did they discuss it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. But you must remember what-- first of all, in 1938, many Jews came to my hometown from Germany. They were expelled from Germany, because they were actually born in Poland. And at that time, the Poles were not so willing to take them in. And many young members of the Zionist organization went through the border and tried, in very different ways, to get them in. There were some that came to my hometown. And one of the families lived down our street. And the girl of that family became a very close friend of mine. But as the war was imminent, and we realized that sooner or later, something will happen, my father, at that time-- also, there was talk about a war with Russia and stuff like that. And the people of my hometown were hoping that if a war comes into being, that it would be-- and Poland should lose or whatever-- it's better Germany than Russia. For they remembered the condition under Russia from World War I. Of course, they had no idea, no inkling, of what Germany would become to us Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever listen to Hitler's speeches on the radio? Or can you remember your parents discussing the Nuremberg Laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Before the war, not much-- not much. We were not that informed of all the details of what German-- our radios did not speak of that very much. And the newspapers did not write too much about it. And shortly before the war, oddly enough, in Poland, instead of getting ready for the war, the government officials were sending out people to check which houses need renewal, which houses need painting-- to beautify their street. Our street was beautiful. It had flowers in the center. And somehow-- maybe because I was still too young-- I did not feel the effect of the oncoming war until almost shortly before. When my-- when we knew already that the war was imminent, my father and mother decided that because we were so close to the German border, that we should move inward. There was a train leaving the next morning. And we took with us just the necessities, locked up the house, and we all went on a train to Kielce to be with my sister. I had two married sisters by then. One lived in my hometown, my sister Regina. The oldest one, she was already married and had a three-year-old little girl. And my sister Sasha got married shortly before the war and lived in Kielce. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that?

speaker: interviewee
text: K-I-E-L-C-E. It was more inland. And we tried to get there by the train to be away from the border. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this? In '38?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1939 already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember the month?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Summer?

speaker: interviewee
text: September-- beginning-- I mean, end of August or beginning-- very beginning of September. It must have been then. And as we were moving, the train was moving, we were soon overtaken by the German air force. Because we heard the bombers overhead, and as they were dropping the bombs, the train would stop. We would disembark. A few minutes later, it would start, and we would go on. At the next step, we realized that my brother, Romek, was no longer on the train. What happened-- he and some other neighbors-- I don't know if they didn't make the train or whatever-- they returned home. So we went on. We were stopped-- soon overran by the German armies, and kept there for several days until we were allowed to return home.

speaker: interviewer
text: Kept where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in that small, little town in which-- by-- by that time, which the-- the train reached.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you sleep?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we slept-- I-- I don't even recall. It was like a huge room. I don't know. It must have been some kind of a hall. It was strange. And we slept there one night. And next morning, we were forced to return, by foot, home. And we did. It took us a long time. But then again, we didn't get very far from my hometown.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now your brother suddenly disappeared. He was only 13, wasn't he?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- no. He was almost 15 at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he and several men returned home. And until today, we don't know if he couldn't make the train or if he just wanted to go back. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when we returned home, a horrible shock hit us. It was then, on September 4th-- that was when the Nazis entered my home town. And it was when we returned home that we felt the horrible impact of the war. For it was then that we found out that my only brother was shot. And we found out what happened. As they entered my hometown-- and my hometown, since being so close to the border, they actually entered through my street-- through our street. And as they entered, they immediately tried to terrorize the people, or frighten them, whatever. They approached certain buildings, especially one building where my aunt lived, and hollered out. And I found this out from my aunt, who survived that-- that they hollered out [GERMAN]-- all Jewish males come forward. And from that building, 13 men came forth-- my uncle, my cousin, my brother, who was in my aunt's house, obviously, and my best girlfriend's father and two brothers-- all in all, 13 young men and-- men. And they were marched to the edge of town. They were ordered to dig a ditch. And of course, they obeyed, because first of all, that was the first day of the war. No one knew what was planned for the Jewish people. And then having machine gun standing over you, you obey. When they finished, they were shot and buried in the mass grave. I'm certain that in his death, my brother did not know why he was dying. He was 15 years old. He died only because he was a Jew, and for no other reason at all. You see, this was the very first day of the war. Had the people of my hometown known what was to follow-- had they had an idea, even-- they would have risen further back when we were still united, when our leaders were among us, when the strength was there. But after all, this was the 20th century.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who could even believe that the Nazis, the German people, the highly cultural German nation, the people who are so highly educated-- not barbarians from a primitive, backward country-- but Germany-- with the best-educated scientists, with the Wagner music, with Beethoven's, and all that-- who would believe that there was a definite plan at work? We didn't. Well, when we found out where that-- about the death of my brother-- I cannot tell you. I cannot explain what a terrible shock this was to us, especially to my father. You know, in the Jewish religion, it is the son who says the kaddish, or the prayer, after his father dies. Can you imagine how difficult it must have been for my father to have to say the kaddish after his one and only son? I can't imagine how he could endure that pain, how painful that must have been to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: But it was then, in December-- of September, 1939, that I realized that life will never be the same for me. But of course, little did I know that this was only the beginning of things to come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the family sit shiva the way they would have done if a normal death?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't recall, because that wasn't immediately. Because he didn't have a regular burial place. We didn't even know exactly where. And I don't remember if they did or not. But if-- if that was required, I'm sure that they did. I-- I did not. I don't remember sitting shiva because in school--

speaker: interviewer
text: You just went to school and-- yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I went and returned to school. And-- but our home was never the same again. Instead of songs, it was still. It was quiet. It was a sad place. It was nothing like the home that I remembered from before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What was happening then? Things were becoming more difficult each day. Each day, new ordinances-- Such as?

speaker: interviewee
text: --were passed upon us. It started with the white armband with the Star of David on it. Then it was changed to a yellow star with the word Jude-- the Jew-- on the front and on the back of your clothing. We were barred from certain sections of the city. We were forbidden to enter a theater or a movie house. We could not travel on a train or a streetcar. You see, what was happening, it was happening very gradually, very systematically. And we were being conditioned. But slowly, our liberties were being taken away from us one at a time. And with each of the liberties that we gave up, we thought-- or hoped-- that perhaps now they will leave us alone. Soon, Jewish property was being confiscated, and so on. Because who could possibly, possibly have imagined or believed that things will get that much worse to the extent-- you see, the Nazis had a way of deceiving us. They deceived us all the way to the gas chambers. I cannot possibly imagine today that if somebody would have come and told us that there is such a plan that anyone would even have believed it, that it was possible. Because this is inconceivable until today, to believe that highly cultural nation can carry out such horrendous crimes. In December of 1939, I was in school, and I was a happy child. We-- the school-- the building that I was going to school to was a brand new. They were building it in 1938. It was a beautiful building. As a matter of fact, I remember we were not allowed to wear shoes to school. We had to change in school into a special shoe, because the floors were so gorgeous. It was brand new building, and we were so proud of it. And especially the gym room was so different, so new. We were so proud of that school. And I adored my teacher, Helen Bergman. And I loved school. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old were you in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 9 years old. And I was in school. And then an announcement came that from that day on, no Jewish child could attend school.

speaker: interviewer
text: But it was a Jewish school.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a Jewish school, yes. But nevertheless, schools suddenly were closed to us. And I cannot begin to describe you what a horrible feeling this is for a child.

speaker: interviewer
text: First, we thought it was a temporary ordinance. Soon, it will be revoked. I mean, how could you even understand that children would not be allowed-- would be forbidden to attend school? Can we imagine a 9, 10-year-old child stand in front of the house in the morning and observe all my Christian friends who are going to school, carrying books, laughing? And there I stood, helpless, not even understanding why I am not allowed to go to school. But it was a very painful feeling.

speaker: interviewee
text: But of course, I didn't realize then, in 1939, that for the next six years-- duration of the war-- and two years after, I would never, never be allowed to go to school. And I cannot even describe to you what a horrible feeling this is. I loved school. I still love school.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, from the beginning, my mother decided to take a tutor, because we were hoping the schools would open up. And [NON-ENGLISH] Klein-- he lived in a building near us, and he began teaching us. As a matter of fact, he was teaching us German and some English. And I'll never forget the table, the lamp. And I am sorry that I don't have the little book. It's a memorial book, like-- a tiny one. And I still have it, but I have it in Cleveland. And on the first page, he wrote, 'Work while you work, and play while you play, for this is the way to be happy and gay.' And underneath, he wrote in Polish, 'Always remember this, [NON-ENGLISH].' And it was 1940. And the museum-- I should give it to. And I will give it to them someday. But I can't part with it yet. But soon, of course, our money was running out.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because our store was confiscated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was just assigned to work in a co-operative store, which was a store where people would come only with food coupons and purchase.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now explain about-- they confiscated the store. What happened to it?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. The store was confiscated. They took away our store from us. We no longer owned the store. First, they closed it up, and they closed up many stores. And they just assigned certain stores where Jews could shop. But you had to have special coupons for food. And my father was assigned to work in one of those co-operative stores, like. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was-- our money, of course, was running out. We didn't have much-- just barely for food. So we-- my mother could not afford to pay for our tutor any longer. So we began teaching each other. Like my older sister would help me. My younger sister, I would help. And in turn, we would try to continue to learn whatever and as much as we possibly could. But it wasn't very much under these conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, life was becoming much more difficult as time went on. In 1940, end of 1940, the Nazi raids began. The Nazis would come. You know, they were careful to keep the dates of the implementation of their policies secret. They would come suddenly without any warning, surround a section of town, and would round up people. Or what was even worse-- they would round them up, take them away, and we would not know what they did with them. And most of the time, they would send the young people to labor camps, deep in Germany. Or--

speaker: interviewer
text: I think we should-- we should continue on the next tape. You will tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
